name: Ordinary Differential Equations
tagline: Four patterns cover most quant interview ODEs: separable, change-of-variable, first-order linear (integrating factor), and second-order constant-coefficient (characteristic equation).

blocks.text: Most ODEs you'll see in a quant interview reduce to one of four canonical patterns. (1) Separable: dy/dx = g(x) * h(y) -- separate the variables and integrate. (2) Change-of-variable: not separable in the original variables, but a substitution makes it separable. (3) First-order linear: dy/dx + P(x) y = Q(x) -- multiply by an integrating factor I(x) = e^(integral P dx). (4) Second-order homogeneous with constant coefficients: solve the characteristic equation a*r^2 + b*r + c = 0 to get the form of the general solution.

For nonhomogeneous a*y'' + b*y' + c*y = d(x), the general solution is y_h + y_p where y_h is the homogeneous solution and y_p is any particular solution. When d(x) is a polynomial, try y_p as a polynomial of the same degree and match coefficients.

blocks.label: Mental model
blocks.text: First, identify the pattern. Separable? Easy: integrate both sides. Linear? Easy: integrating factor. Homogeneous constant-coefficient? Easy: characteristic equation. Otherwise: try a substitution. The hard step is RECOGNIZING which pattern applies.

whenToUse: Modeling growth, decay, and rates of change.
Pricing problems where a variable evolves continuously (e.g., the Black-Scholes PDE reduces to ODEs in some variables).
Probability problems that admit a continuous-time formulation.
Anywhere a function and its rate of change satisfy a relation.
recipe: Try to recognize the pattern: separable / linear / homogeneous-constant-coefficient / nonhomogeneous.
Separable: integrate dy/h(y) = g(x) dx. Apply initial conditions.
First-order linear: compute I(x) = e^(integral P dx), multiply through, integrate (I*y)' = I*Q.

Second-order homogeneous: solve a*r^2 + b*r + c = 0. Real distinct roots give c1*e^(r1 x) + c2*e^(r2 x); real repeated give (c1 + c2*x)*e^(r x); complex give e^(alpha x) * (c1*cos(beta x) + c2*sin(beta x)).
Nonhomogeneous: add the homogeneous solution to ANY particular solution. Try a guess of the same form as d(x).
commonPitfalls: Forgetting the integration constant on indefinite integrals.
In separable ODEs: dividing by h(y) without checking h(y) != 0 (you may miss the constant solution where h vanishes).
In integrating-factor problems: not simplifying I(x)*P(x)*y term as (I(x)*y)' before integrating.
In characteristic-equation problems: confusing real-distinct, real-repeated, and complex cases when the discriminant is small.
When d(x) is a polynomial that resembles a homogeneous solution, the standard particular-solution guess fails; multiply by x to lift it out of the resonance.
